criteria: The agent has read Section 2.1 completely to understand the Gravity Spy dataset construction, including the Omicron pipeline trigger filtering (SNR > 7.5, peak frequency 10–2048 Hz) and the 23-class classification task.
The agent has read Table 1 to understand the distribution of all 23 glitch classes across the training (8439), validation (1687), and testing (3538) splits.
The agent has read Section 2.2 to understand the three multi-view fusion strategies (early fusion, late fusion, and intermediate fusion) and their respective advantages and limitations.
The agent has read Section 2.3 to understand the proposed classifier architecture, including the inception residual block, gated attention module, and label smoothing technique.
The agent has read Figure 4 to understand the per-branch architecture: Pre-Conv Block → 3×(Conv Block + Incep-Res Block) → Post-Conv Block → Dropout → Flatten → FC → Attention Module.
The agent has read Section 2.4 to understand the statistical analysis methodology, including 5 independent runs, paired t-test (p < 0.05), reliability plots, and ECE.
The agent has read Table 2 to understand the fusion strategy comparison results on the O3 test set.
The agent has read Figure 5 to understand how model performance varies across smoothing levels β from 0 to 1.
The agent has read Table 3 to understand the comparison of gated attention vs. max pooling vs. mean pooling variants.
The agent has read Table 4 to understand the class-wise and overall performance comparison between the proposed classifier and the previous Gravity Spy classifier.
The plan includes downloading and loading the Gravity Spy O3 dataset (spectrograms and classification labels) from the Zenodo repository.
The plan includes the data filtering criterion: only retain glitches with consensus confidence scores above 0.9 from both machine-learning models and volunteer classifications.
The plan includes the image preprocessing pipeline: cropping, resizing to 448×448×3, and min-max normalization.
The plan includes implementation of all three fusion strategies (early, late, and intermediate) for comparison experiments.
The plan includes implementing the inception residual block with four parallel convolutional branches and a skip connection.
The plan includes implementing the gated attention module with trainable parameters V, U, and w and the softmax-normalized attention weights.
The plan includes implementing label smoothing with class-weighted cross-entropy loss as defined in Equations (3) and (4).
The plan includes configuring the Adam optimizer with initial learning rate 2×10⁻⁵ and early stopping with patience of 10 epochs.
The plan includes running 5 independent experiments per configuration with different random weight initializations and mini-batch orderings.
The plan includes all ablation studies: comparing fusion strategies, sweeping β from 0 to 1 in 0.1 increments, and comparing attention vs. pooling variants.
The plan includes retraining the previous Gravity Spy classifier from scratch on the same training dataset for a fair comparison.
The plan includes t-SNE visualization of 128-dimensional latent features reduced to 2D, and reliability plot / ECE evaluation.
The code for loading the Gravity Spy O3 spectrograms (four time-window images: 0.5 s, 1.0 s, 2.0 s, 4.0 s per glitch) from the Zenodo dataset has been implemented.
The code for filtering the dataset to only include glitches with consensus confidence > 0.9 from both ML models and volunteer classifications has been implemented.
The code for splitting the dataset into non-overlapping training (8439), validation (1687), and testing (3538) subsets with no data leakage has been implemented.
The code for cropping each spectrogram image using bounding box [top-left vertical: 60, top-left horizontal: 100, height: 580, width: 675] has been implemented.
The code for resizing all cropped spectrogram images to 448×448×3 using bilinear interpolation has been implemented.
The code for min-max normalization of pixel intensities using (X − X_min) / (X_max − X_min) to scale each image to the range [0, 1] has been implemented.
The code for early fusion has been implemented by constructing a 2h×2w merged image by juxtaposing the four h×w time-window view images, feeding it into a single CNN.
The code for late fusion has been implemented with four separate CNN models (one per time window), each producing predictions that are aggregated (e.g., averaging) at the decision level.
The code for the intermediate fusion architecture with four parallel branches (one per time window) that extract features separately and fuse at the feature level has been implemented.
The code for the convolutional block (Pre-Conv, Conv, and Post-Conv) consisting of a convolutional layer, ReLU activation, batch normalization, and max pooling has been implemented.
The code for the inception residual block with four parallel branches using 1×1, 1×1→3×3, 1×1→5×5 convolutions, and max pooling has been implemented.
The code for the dimensionality-reduction 1×1 convolution applied before the 3×3 and 5×5 convolutions inside the inception residual block has been implemented.

The code for the skip connection in the inception residual block, where concatenated branch outputs are combined with the original input via a 1×1 conv and element-wise addition followed by ReLU, has been implemented.
The code for repeating the (Conv Block + Incep-Res Block) combination exactly 3 times within each branch before the Post-Conv Block has been implemented.
The code for the flatten layer followed by the fully-connected layer that reduces each branch's feature maps to M=128 dimensional latent vectors z_i has been implemented.
The code for the attention module aggregation as Equation (1): M_Att = sum_{i=1}^{4} α_i z_i has been implemented.

The code for computing the gated attention weights α_i using Equation (2), combining tanh and sigmoid activations with element-wise multiplication (⊙) before softmax normalization, has been implemented.
The code for the trainable attention parameters V ∈ R^{L×M}, U ∈ R^{L×M}, and w ∈ R^{L×1} with L=64 and M=128 has been implemented.
The code for label smoothing as Equation (3): q = (1 − β)y + βu, where y is the one-hot label and u is a uniform distribution over 23 classes, has been implemented.
The code for the weighted cross-entropy loss function as Equation (4): L(y, ŷ) = −(1/C) Σ γ_i q_i log(ŷ_i) with class weights γ_i has been implemented.
The code for computing class weights γ_i that are inversely proportional to the number of instances per class and sum to 1 across all 23 classes has been implemented.
The code for applying L2 regularization to each convolutional layer in the network has been implemented.
The code for applying dropout after each fully-connected layer to prevent overfitting has been implemented.
The Adam optimizer is configured with an initial learning rate of 2×10⁻⁵ for training the proposed model.
Early stopping with patience=10 is implemented, halting training when validation accuracy does not improve for 10 consecutive epochs.
The final fully-connected layer followed by softmax activation producing a 23-class probability distribution has been implemented.
The max pooling and mean pooling variants (without the attention module) of the intermediate fusion network have been implemented as ablation baselines.
The previous Gravity Spy classifier architecture (from Soni et al. 2021 / [24]) has been implemented and can be retrained from scratch on the same Gravity Spy O3 training dataset.
The code for computing accuracy, precision, F1 score, and AUC on the held-out test set has been implemented.
The code for performing a paired t-test to assess statistical significance (p < 0.05) between pairs of model results has been implemented.
The code for t-SNE dimensionality reduction from M=128 to 2 dimensions on the latent feature vectors extracted after the attention module has been implemented.
The code for generating reliability plots (observed fraction of positives vs. predicted fraction of positives across confidence bins) has been implemented.
The code for computing the Expected Calibration Error (ECE) as the average deviation between predicted probabilities and true likelihood has been implemented.
The code for visualizing per-view attention weights alongside their corresponding spectrogram images (as in Figure 6) has been implemented.
All three fusion strategy models (early, late, intermediate) have been trained and evaluated on the O3 test set using 5 independent runs each.
The label smoothing ablation experiments with β values from 0 to 1 in increments of 0.1 (11 total configurations) have been executed successfully.
The attention mechanism comparison experiments (gated attention, max pooling, mean pooling) using the intermediate fusion architecture have been executed successfully with 5 independent runs each.
Both the proposed classifier and the retrained previous Gravity Spy classifier have been evaluated on the O3 test set, generating accuracy, precision, F1, AUC, and class-wise accuracy results.
The t-SNE visualization and reliability plots for both classifiers have been generated and saved successfully.
The trained models have been applied to the early O4 glitch dataset (2267 glitches from 24 May 2023 to 19 September 2023) to analyze confidence distributions.
All experiments have been executed with 5 independent runs per configuration, and mean ± standard deviation values are reported for each metric.
The execution outcomes match the results in Table 2: the intermediate fusion model achieves accuracy of 0.941±0.021, precision of 0.950±0.010, F1 of 0.931±0.015, and AUC of 0.965±0.004.
The execution outcomes match the results in Table 2: the intermediate fusion model significantly outperforms the early fusion model across all four metrics (p < 0.05).
The execution outcomes match the results in Table 2: the performance difference between early fusion and late fusion is not statistically significant (p ≥ 0.05).
The execution outcomes match Figure 5: the optimal smoothing parameter is β=0.3, achieving the best accuracy, precision, F1, and AUC simultaneously.
The execution outcomes match Figure 5: label smoothing with β ≤ 0.5 consistently outperforms no smoothing (β=0), while β > 0.5 leads to degraded performance.
The execution outcomes match Table 3: the gated attention model significantly outperforms both max pooling and mean pooling variants (p < 0.05) across all four metrics.
The execution outcomes match Table 3: there is no statistically significant difference in performance between the max pooling and mean pooling variants.

The execution outcomes match Table 4: the proposed classifier outperforms the previous classifier by 2.7–3.7% on overall accuracy (0.941 vs. 0.906), precision (0.950 vs. 0.913), F1 (0.931 vs. 0.904), and AUC (0.965 vs. 0.937).
The execution outcomes match Table 4: the proposed classifier shows the largest accuracy improvements for Wandering Line (0.520→0.740), Repeating Blips (0.772→0.933), and Helix (0.732→0.891).
The execution outcomes match Figure 8: the proposed model achieves a lower Expected Calibration Error (ECE) of 0.06 compared to the previous model's ECE of 0.09.

The execution outcomes match Figure 10: the previous model exhibits confidence overfitting (most O4 glitch class predictions have confidence near 1), while the proposed model shows varying confidence ranges across different glitch classes.
